title: The Space Race Comes to Latin America - Americas Quarterly
gist: This is an issue table-of-contents page for AQ's Latin America space-race special report, bundling articles on the region's new ALCE space agency, Satellogic, Brazil's and Argentina's satellite programs, plus separate pieces on Brazil's October elections and the region's liberal-order erosion and prison boom.

insights.claim: The issue frames Latin America's ambition as wanting to be 'not just a battleground, but a protagonist' in the burgeoning space economy, per the lead editorial by Juan Pablo Toro.
insights.speaker: JUAN PABLO TORO
insights.so_what: Sets the organizing frame for the whole special report: regional agency in the space economy, not passive great-power battleground status.
insights.quote: not just a battleground, but a protagonist
insights.locator: The Space Race Comes to Latin America

insights.claim: Latin America's regional space agency ALCE held its first general assembly in Mexico in February but faces open questions about funding and political buy-in.
insights.speaker: JUAN PABLO TORO
insights.so_what: A newly convened regional space agency with unresolved funding is an early-stage institution to track for whether it gains real budget authority or stays symbolic.
insights.locator: Latin America Has a Space Agency. Now Comes the Hard Part

insights.claim: Satellogic, a company founded in Buenos Aires, is described as pushing competitors to rethink who owns the sky.
insights.speaker: JUAN PABLO TORO
insights.so_what: Satellogic is flagged as a notable Latin American-origin commercial space company challenging incumbents — a name to research further for regional space-sector exposure.
insights.locator: How Satellogic Is Trying to Reshape the Space Race

insights.claim: Amazônia-1 is described as the first satellite entirely designed and operated by Brazil, part of a larger Brazilian satellite mission.
insights.speaker: JUAN PABLO TORO
insights.so_what: Brazil's fully domestic satellite program (Amazônia-1) is a concrete proof point of the country's growing indigenous space capability.

insights.claim: Argentina's national space agency's satellite program is described as a rare example of sustained strategic investment in the country.
insights.speaker: JUAN PABLO TORO
insights.so_what: Argentina's satellite program stands out for policy continuity in a country otherwise known for economic volatility — worth benchmarking against other Argentine industrial policy.

themes.theme: Other regional political themes in this issue

insights.claim: A former president (Carlos Alvarado Quesada) argues climate has not disappeared from Latin America's political agenda but persists indirectly, emerging within other topics like crime and mining.
insights.speaker: Carlos Alvarado Quesada
insights.so_what: Signals that climate-linked risk in the region should be tracked through adjacent proxies (crime, mining policy) rather than as a standalone headline issue.
insights.locator: Despite Appearances, Climate Has Not Disappeared from the Agenda

insights.claim: One featured piece argues Latin American governments are building massive prison facilities to contain a growing inmate population, but warns the strategy could backfire.
insights.speaker: Robert Muggah
insights.so_what: Flags mass incarceration as a policy trend with acknowledged backfire risk — relevant to security/governance risk assessments in the region.
insights.locator: The Problem with Latin America's Prison Boom

insights.claim: A co-authored piece argues that citizens and leaders across the Americas are abandoning the rules of liberal democracy, framing the liberal order as unraveling in the region.
insights.speaker: Andrés Malamud, Juan Pablo Luna, Alberto Vergara
insights.so_what: A direct thesis of democratic backsliding across the Americas — a macro political-risk theme relevant to sovereign and institutional risk assessments region-wide.
insights.locator: Why the Liberal Order Is Unraveling in the Americas
